# Agent-host seams â€” shared lifecycle core vs host-specific adapters

The durable map of where shipit's agent-host boundary runs (CDX01 \#627). Two coordinator hosts exist today â€” Claude Code and Codex â€” and every future host lands against the same line: the **lifecycle** is ONE shared implementation, and each host contributes only a set of NARROW adapters at named seams. This doc names those seams so Claude/Codex/other-agent work never forks a parallel implementation of something the core already owns.

The rule in one line: if two hosts would each need a copy of some logic, that logic belongs in the shared core behind a seam; a host adapter carries only what is genuinely different about that host (its config file format, its launch mechanics, its payload/env shape, its CLI argv and auth posture).

## 1. The shared lifecycle core (host-agnostic â€” one implementation)

These modules are the same code no matter which host is driving. A host NEVER gets its own variant of any of them; it reaches them through the adapters in section 2.

Tree creation

: `src/shipit/tree/` â€” the pure planner (`layout.py`) plus the orchestrator (`create.py`), ADR-0014/ADR-0027. Every Tree of every shape â€” session, write, review â€” is minted here. The Claude WorktreeCreate hook and `shipit session codex` call the SAME planner and orchestrator; neither host carries parallel Tree code.

Install reconcile

: `src/shipit/install/` â€” the managed-unit catalog (`units.py`), the reconcile, the apply. Host adapters are **delivered** by it (the `.claude/` and `.codex/` units, the launchers), but the reconcile machinery itself knows nothing host-specific: a Codex hook entry is just one more JSON-hook unit through the same splice as a Claude one.

PR engine

: `src/shipit/prstate/` and the `shipit pr status` / `shipit pr next` verbs. PRs have no host axis at all â€” the engine is the same decider regardless of which agent CLI opened or shepherds the PR.

Hook verbs

: `src/shipit/verbs/hook/` â€” `sessionstart`, `pretooluse`, `worktreecreate`, the stop/subagent-stop eval verbs. These carry the lifecycle LOGIC (activation, the coordinator edit guard, Tree provisioning, eval recording). Hosts differ only in how a verb gets invoked and what payload shape arrives â€” that adaptation lives in the managed hook **entries** (section 2), never in the verbs.

Role policy

: `src/shipit/harness/` â€” Role Profile registry, role prompts, the coordinator/subagent policy split, and codepath detection (ADR-0011/ADR-0038/ADR-0047). A Role means the same thing in every host: its checkout strategy, enforcement posture, supported launch contexts, and result channel come from the fixed registry, never from a host adapter or consumer config.

Work Env

: `src/shipit/workenv.py` â€” the resolved execution context every launch boundary consumes: WorkingDir, optional Tree provenance, checkout strategy, optional pixi Activation / EnvIdentity, and routing (`pixi-run`, `activation-snapshot`, or `ambient`). Work Env names WHERE work runs and WITH WHICH activation; it does not execute. Exec remains the process seam, pixi owns activation and environment identity, and host adapters only decide how their CLI argv and auth posture reach that shared value.

Liveness

: `src/shipit/session/liveness.py` â€” one pidfile contract for whichever coordinator CLI owns a session Tree; both backends' session Trees ride the same ephemeral gc ladder.

Backend registry

: `src/shipit/agent/backend.py` â€” the ONE closed identity registry (ADR-0025): each backend's canonical name and every alias (spawn token, funnel names, credential keys, model aliases) defined once and referenced everywhere else.

Launcher common path

: `agent-start` â€” the generic managed launcher (`./agent-start <agent> [args...]`). Usage, the host dispatch, the CLI presence check, and arg forwarding are written once; a change to the common start path cannot be forgotten for one agent.

## 2. The host-specific adapters (narrow, named, one per seam)

What a host is ALLOWED to own. Each adapter is small, lives in one place, and is delivered/reconciled by the shared install machinery.

**Adapter matrix**

| Seam                            | Claude Code                                                                                                                                                                                 | Codex                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Managed host config             | `.claude/settings.json` (five JSON-hook units) + `.claude/agents/` defs                                                                                                                     | `.codex/config.toml` (whole file) + `.codex/hooks.json` (two JSON-hook units)                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| Launch strategy                 | `agent-start claude`: mint `sess-<utc>-<pid>`, exec `claude --worktree <id>` â€” the WorktreeCreate pre-launch seam provisions the session Tree and resolves the coordinator session Work Env | `agent-start codex`: exec the pinned `./bin/shipit session codex` â€” explicit Tree provisioning, Work Env resolution, then exec codex rooted in it                                                                                                                               |
| Hook payload/env adaptation     | Claude supplies the JSON payload on stdin, `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR`, `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE`                                                                                                         | sessionstart entry synthesizes a `{"cwd": <os.getcwd()>}` payload via python; pretooluse entry synthesizes no payload â€” it resolves the git root via `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` and passes codex's native stdin through; session identity rides `SHIPIT_LOG_CTX_*` exports |
| Backend CLI argv / auth posture | `spawn/backends/claude.py` (ADR-0020 adapter \#0)                                                                                                                                           | `spawn/backends/codex.py` + `session/bootstrap.py`: sandbox-bypass posture (the Tree IS the isolation), API-billing keys scrubbed so the ChatGPT sign-in stays first-class, `CODEX_ACCESS_TOKEN` passes through                                                                 |

Launch strategy

: The host axis of "how does a coordinator session start". Claude Code fixes its cwd before any shipit code runs, so its ONE pre-launch seam is the `--worktree` flag firing the WorktreeCreate hook (ADR-0027). Codex has no such seam â€” but shipit launches the codex process itself, so `shipit session codex` (`src/shipit/verbs/session.py`, the pure core in `src/shipit/session/bootstrap.py`) provisions first and execs second. `agent-start` encodes exactly this fork as its host strategy table: one row plus one narrow launch function per host, never a conditional scattered through the common path.

Managed host config

: Where the host reads its project-level wiring from. The Claude units splice shipit's entries into `.claude/settings.json`; the Codex layer is `.codex/config.toml` + `.codex/hooks.json` (\#603). Both route to the SAME `./bin/shipit hook ...` verbs â€” the entries differ only in the payload/env adaptation above, and both reconcile through the same JSON-hook splice.

Backend CLI argv / auth posture

: How a subagent Run (or the coordinator exec) actually invokes the host CLI: `src/shipit/spawn/backends/` holds one launch adapter per backend (ADR-0020), reading names/models off the shared identity registry. Auth posture (what env is scrubbed, what passes through) is defined ONCE on the adapter and reused by the session launch. Role Profile preflight happens before any Tree provisioning or backend launch; the backend adapter never decides whether a Role is allowed in a launch context.

## 3. Rules for adding a host

A new agent host is wired by adding adapters at the named seams â€” and nothing else. Concretely:

1.  Add ONE identity entry to the closed backend registry
    (`src/shipit/agent/backend.py`, ADR-0025) â€” canonical name and every alias, defined once.
2.  Add ONE launch adapter under `src/shipit/spawn/backends/`
    (ADR-0020) so `shipit spawn subagent --backend <host>` can launch role-scoped Runs.
3.  Add ONE row (plus its narrow launch function) to the `agent-start`
    host strategy table. If the host has a Claude-style pre-launch cwd seam, ride it; if not, add a `shipit session <host>` verb that provisions the session Tree explicitly and execs the CLI rooted in it â€” reusing the same Tree planner/orchestrator, never parallel Tree code.
4.  Deliver the host's project-level config as managed units in
    `src/shipit/install/units.py`, reusing the JSON-hook splice where the host file is structured. The hook entries adapt payload/env at the ENTRY and route to the shared `shipit hook` verbs â€” the verbs themselves grow no host conditionals.
5.  Keep the per-host surface behind these seams. If a change would put
    an `if host == ...` into shared lifecycle code or copy logic into a second script, the seam is wrong â€” extend the shared core instead.

The test posture that keeps this honest: shipit-self commits byte-identical copies of every launcher/bootstrap unit (the dogfood reconcile-to-noop guarantee), and drift tests pin the managed entries to shipit's own wiring â€” so a host adapter cannot silently diverge from what `shipit install` distributes.
